file_analyses.file_purpose: Core utilities module providing comprehensive helper functions for the AURA test automation framework. Contains utilities for file operations, Excel manipulation, Playwright browser interactions, screenshot capture, image comparison, validation reporting, flow execution, animation extraction (SVG/GIF), timestamp generation, string sanitization, and various data processing functions. This module serves as the foundation for scraping, validation, and reporting operations across the framework.

imports.import_statement: from typing import Callable, Optional, Dict
from core.settings import Constants, Config, framework_logger

classes.class_purpose: Custom dictionary subclass that allows attribute-style access to dictionary keys. Enables accessing dictionary values using dot notation (e.g., elements.username_input) instead of bracket notation (e.g., elements['username_input']). Raises AttributeError if a key does not exist when accessed as an attribute.

functions.what_it_does: Generates and returns the current timestamp as a formatted string in the pattern YYYY_MM_DD_HH_MM_SS. Uses datetime.now() to get the current date and time, then formats it using strftime with the format '%Y_%m_%d_%H_%M_%S'. This timestamp format is used throughout the framework for naming output files, directories, and run identifiers to ensure uniqueness and chronological ordering.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: The filename string to sanitize by removing invalid characters

functions.what_it_does: Sanitizes a filename string by replacing all invalid filesystem characters with underscores. Uses a regular expression pattern to match characters that are not allowed in filenames on Windows and other operating systems: backslash, forward slash, asterisk, question mark, colon, double quote, less than, greater than, and pipe. Each matched character is replaced with an underscore using re.sub(). Returns the sanitized filename string safe for use in file operations.

parameters.name: length
parameters.description: The length of the random string to generate. Defaults to 6 characters.

functions.what_it_does: Generates a random alphanumeric string of specified length using lowercase letters and digits. Creates a character pool by concatenating string.ascii_lowercase (a-z) and string.digits (0-9). Uses random.choices() to randomly select characters from this pool k times where k equals the length parameter. Joins the selected characters into a single string and returns it. Useful for generating unique identifiers, test data, or temporary names.

parameters.name: text
parameters.description: The text to hash. Can be a string or bytes object.

functions.what_it_does: Generates a SHA256 hash of the provided text and returns the first 12 characters of the hexadecimal digest. If the input is already bytes, uses it directly; otherwise converts the text to bytes using UTF-8 encoding (or empty string if text is None). Computes the SHA256 hash using hashlib.sha256(), converts to hexadecimal string with hexdigest(), and slices the first 12 characters. This shortened hash is used for generating unique but readable identifiers for files and elements.
functions.usage_example: file_hash = hash_text('some content')  # Returns '3a5f8c2b9d1e' (first 12 chars of hash)

parameters.name: dirs
parameters.description: Variable number of directory paths to create

functions.what_it_does: Creates multiple directories if they do not already exist. Accepts a variable number of directory path strings as arguments. Iterates through each provided directory path and calls os.makedirs() with exist_ok=True, which creates the directory and any necessary parent directories without raising an error if the directory already exists. This is a convenience function for ensuring output directories exist before writing files.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: The attribute name being accessed

functions.what_it_does: Magic method that enables attribute-style access to dictionary keys in the ElementsDict class. When an attribute is accessed using dot notation (e.g., obj.key), this method is called. It attempts to return the value associated with the key from the dictionary using self[name]. If the key does not exist in the dictionary, a KeyError is caught and re-raised as an AttributeError with a descriptive message indicating that the ElementsDict object has no such attribute.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: The attribute name being set

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value to assign to the attribute

functions.what_it_does: Magic method that enables setting dictionary keys using attribute-style assignment in the ElementsDict class. When an attribute is assigned using dot notation (e.g., obj.key = value), this method is called. It stores the value in the dictionary using self[name] = value, effectively treating attribute assignment as dictionary key assignment. This allows seamless use of both dictionary and attribute syntax on the same object.

functions.method_signature: def get_or_create_sheet(workbook, sheet_name: str, headers: list = None)

parameters.name: workbook
parameters.description: The openpyxl Workbook object

parameters.name: sheet_name
parameters.description: The name of the sheet to get or create

parameters.name: headers
parameters.description: Optional list of header strings. If None, uses Constants.SCRAPE_HEADERS.

functions.docstring: Return the sheet by name, or create it (with headers) if missing.
functions.what_it_does: Retrieves an existing Excel worksheet by name from a workbook, or creates a new one with headers if it does not exist. First checks if sheet_name exists in workbook.sheetnames. If it exists, returns the existing sheet. If not, creates a new sheet using workbook.create_sheet() with the specified title. Then writes header row by iterating through the headers list (or Constants.SCRAPE_HEADERS if headers is None), writing each header to the first row starting at column 1, and centering the text using Alignment(horizontal='center'). Returns the sheet object.

parameters.name: sheet
parameters.description: The openpyxl worksheet object to search

parameters.name: selector
parameters.description: The selector value to search for in the first column

functions.docstring: Return True if any value in the first column matches 'selector'.
functions.what_it_does: Searches an Excel worksheet for a specific selector value in the first column and returns True if found, False otherwise. Iterates through all rows starting from row 2 (skipping header) using sheet.iter_rows() with min_row=2, max_col=1, and values_only=True to get only the first column values. For each row, unpacks the single cell value and compares it to the selector parameter. Returns True immediately upon finding a match, or False if no match is found after checking all rows. Used to prevent duplicate entries when scraping data.
functions.usage_example: exists = find_row(sheet, '[data-testid="login-btn"]')  # Returns True if selector exists

parameters.name: sheet
parameters.description: The openpyxl worksheet object to append to

parameters.name: row_data
parameters.description: List of values to append as a new row. First element should be the selector.

functions.docstring: Append row_data to the sheet if not already present (by selector).
functions.what_it_does: Appends a row of scraped data to an Excel worksheet only if the selector (first element) is not already present and is not empty. Extracts the selector from row_data[0]. If the selector is empty or None, logs a debug message and returns without appending. Calls find_row() to check if the selector already exists in the sheet. If it exists, logs a debug message about skipping the duplicate and returns. If the selector is new, appends the entire row_data list to the sheet using sheet.append(). This prevents duplicate scraping of the same UI element.

parameters.name: sheet
parameters.description: The openpyxl worksheet object to adjust

functions.docstring: Resize columns based on maximum content length for readability.
functions.what_it_does: Automatically adjusts the width of all columns in an Excel worksheet based on the maximum content length in each column for better readability. Iterates through each column using sheet.columns. For each column, gets the column letter using get_column_letter() from the first cell's column index. Then iterates through all cells in that column, checking each cell's value. If the value is not None, calculates its string length and tracks the maximum length. After processing all cells in the column, sets the column width to max_len + 2 using sheet.column_dimensions[col_letter].width. The +2 adds padding for visual spacing.
functions.usage_example: auto_adjust_column_widths(sheet)  # Adjusts all column widths in the sheet

parameters.name: workbook
parameters.description: The openpyxl Workbook object

parameters.name: sheet_name
parameters.description: The name of the sheet to retrieve

functions.docstring: Return sheet by name or None (with a warning).
functions.what_it_does: Retrieves a worksheet by name from an Excel workbook, returning the sheet object if found or None with a warning if not found. First checks if the workbook exists and if sheet_name is in workbook.sheetnames. If both conditions are true, returns the sheet using workbook[sheet_name]. If the sheet is not found, logs a warning message using framework_logger.warning() indicating that the sheet was not found, and returns None. This provides safe sheet access with logging for debugging.
functions.usage_example: sheet = get_sheet_by_name(wb, 'LoginPage')  # Returns sheet or None

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: File path to the Excel workbook

parameters.name: headers
parameters.description: List of header strings for new workbooks

functions.what_it_does: Loads an existing Excel workbook from the specified path or creates a new one if the file does not exist. First checks if the file exists using os.path.exists(). If it exists, loads the workbook using openpyxl.load_workbook() and gets the active worksheet. If the file does not exist, creates a new Workbook object, removes the default sheet that is automatically created, and sets ws to None. Returns a tuple of (workbook, worksheet). The headers parameter is provided for consistency but not used in this function - it would be used by calling code when creating sheets.

parameters.name: wb
parameters.description: The openpyxl Workbook object to save

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: File path where the workbook should be saved

functions.what_it_does: Saves an Excel workbook to the specified file path, creating the directory structure if it does not exist. Extracts the directory path from the full file path using os.path.dirname(). If the directory path is not empty, creates all necessary parent directories using os.makedirs() with exist_ok=True. Then saves the workbook to the specified path using wb.save(). This ensures that the workbook can be saved even if the target directory does not exist yet.

parameters.name: excel_path
parameters.description: Path to the Excel file containing context data

functions.what_it_does: Extracts and returns a sorted list of unique context names from an Excel file. Loads the workbook using openpyxl.load_workbook() and gets the active worksheet. Creates an empty set called contexts to store unique context names. Iterates through all rows starting from row 2 (skipping header) using ws.iter_rows() with values_only=True. For each row, unpacks the first two values as flow and context (ignoring the rest with *_). If both flow and context are not empty, adds the context to the set. After processing all rows, converts the set to a sorted list and returns it. This is used to discover all unique test contexts in a test data file.

parameters.name: elem
parameters.description: Playwright ElementHandle to check

functions.what_it_does: Checks if a Playwright element is hidden or disabled by evaluating JavaScript in the browser context. Uses elem.evaluate() to execute JavaScript that checks two conditions: whether el.offsetParent is null (which indicates the element is hidden via CSS display:none or visibility:hidden) or whether el.disabled is true (which indicates a form element is disabled). Returns True if either condition is met, False otherwise. This is more reliable than just checking visibility as it also detects CSS-hidden elements.
functions.usage_example: is_hidden = is_element_hidden_or_disabled(element_handle)  # Returns True if hidden or disabled

parameters.name: elem
parameters.description: Playwright ElementHandle to get position from

functions.what_it_does: Retrieves the position and dimensions of a Playwright element as a dictionary. Calls elem.bounding_box() to get the element's bounding box, which returns a dictionary with x, y, width, and height keys, or None if the element is not visible. Uses the or operator with an empty dict as fallback if bounding_box returns None. Constructs and returns a new dictionary with integer values for x, y, width, and height by using dict.get() with default value 0 for each key and converting to int. This ensures the function always returns a valid dictionary even if the element has no bounding box.

parameters.name: elem
parameters.description: Playwright ElementHandle to extract styles from

functions.what_it_does: Extracts computed CSS styles from a Playwright element by executing JavaScript in the browser context. Uses elem.evaluate() to run a JavaScript function that gets the computed styles using window.getComputedStyle(el). Constructs and returns a dictionary containing font_family, font_size, font_weight, color, background (backgroundColor), and text_align properties. Each property uses the logical OR operator to return an empty string if the style property is null or undefined. This captures the actual rendered styles of the element as seen in the browser, not just the inline styles.

parameters.name: elem
parameters.description: Playwright ElementHandle to extract text from

functions.what_it_does: Extracts the visible inner text from a Playwright element and returns it as a trimmed string. Calls elem.inner_text() to get the text content of the element as it appears to the user (excluding hidden elements and including line breaks). Then calls strip() to remove leading and trailing whitespace. Returns the cleaned text string. This is preferred over textContent for getting user-visible text as it respects CSS visibility and display properties.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

functions.what_it_does: Disables scrolling on a web page by injecting CSS that sets overflow to hidden on html and body elements. Calls page.add_style_tag() with content parameter containing CSS rule 'html, body { overflow: hidden !important; }'. The !important flag ensures this rule overrides any existing overflow styles. This is useful when capturing screenshots or scraping elements to prevent the page from scrolling during automation, which could cause elements to move or become obscured.
functions.usage_example: freeze_scroll(page)  # Disables scrolling on the page

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

functions.what_it_does: Re-enables scrolling on a web page by injecting CSS that sets overflow to auto on html and body elements. Calls page.add_style_tag() with content parameter containing CSS rule 'html, body { overflow: auto !important; }'. The !important flag ensures this rule overrides the previous freeze_scroll CSS. This restores normal scrolling behavior after it was disabled, allowing the page to scroll naturally again. Should be called after freeze_scroll when scrolling needs to be restored.
functions.usage_example: unfreeze_scroll(page)  # Re-enables scrolling on the page

functions.method_signature: def take_element_screenshot(page: Page, selector: str, output_path: str) -> bool

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

parameters.name: selector
parameters.description: CSS selector or other locator string for the element

parameters.name: output_path
parameters.description: File path where the screenshot should be saved

functions.what_it_does: Captures a screenshot of a specific element on the page identified by a selector and saves it to the specified path. Wraps the operation in a try-except block. Locates the element using page.locator(selector). Checks if the element count is 0 or if the element is not visible using is_visible(), returning False if either condition is true. Extracts the directory path from output_path and creates it if it does not exist using os.makedirs() with exist_ok=True. Calls element.screenshot(path=output_path) to capture and save the screenshot. Returns True on success. If any exception occurs, logs a warning with the selector and error message using framework_logger.warning() and returns False.

functions.method_signature: def take_element_handle_screenshot(handle: ElementHandle, output_path: str) -> bool

parameters.name: handle
parameters.description: Playwright ElementHandle object

parameters.name: output_path
parameters.description: File path where the screenshot should be saved

functions.what_it_does: Captures a screenshot of a specific element using an ElementHandle and saves it to the specified path. Wraps the operation in a try-except block. First checks if the element is visible using handle.is_visible(), returning False if not visible. Extracts the directory path from output_path and creates it if it does not exist using os.makedirs() with exist_ok=True. Calls handle.screenshot(path=output_path) to capture and save the screenshot. Returns True on success. If any exception occurs, logs a warning with the error message using framework_logger.warning() and returns False. This is similar to take_element_screenshot but works with an already-obtained ElementHandle instead of a selector.

functions.method_signature: def save_full_page_screenshot(page: Page, flow_name: str, context_name: str, output_dir: str = None) -> str

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

parameters.name: flow_name
parameters.description: Name of the test flow

parameters.name: context_name
parameters.description: Name of the test context/checkpoint

parameters.name: output_dir
parameters.description: Optional output directory. If None, uses Constants.VALIDATE_OUTPUT_DIR.

functions.what_it_does: Captures a full-page screenshot and saves it with a timestamped filename. Determines the output directory using the provided output_dir parameter or Constants.VALIDATE_OUTPUT_DIR as fallback. Creates the directory if it does not exist using os.makedirs() with exist_ok=True. Generates a filename by combining flow_name, context_name, and current timestamp from get_current_timestamp(), then sanitizes it using sanitize_filename() and appends '.png' extension. Constructs the full path by joining the directory and filename. Calls page.screenshot() with path parameter and full_page=True to capture the entire scrollable page. Returns the filename (not the full path). This is used for validation checkpoints to capture the complete page state.

functions.method_signature: def highlight_validation(ws, row_cells, passed: bool = None)

parameters.name: ws
parameters.description: The openpyxl worksheet object

parameters.name: row_cells
parameters.description: List of cell objects representing a row

parameters.name: passed
parameters.description: Optional boolean indicating pass/fail status (not used in current implementation)

functions.what_it_does: Applies color highlighting to validation result rows in an Excel worksheet based on Text_Result and Screenshot_Result column values. First extracts the header row to build a mapping of column names to indices. Defines a normalize function that converts cell values to uppercase stripped strings. Gets the normalized values of Text_Result and Screenshot_Result columns. If both are empty or N/A, returns without highlighting. Then applies complex conditional logic to determine the appropriate fill color from Constants: PASS (both pass), PASS_TEXT_ONLY (text pass, screenshot N/A), PASS_WITH_EXCLUDES (pass with exclusions), or FAIL (any failure). Finally applies the determined fill color to all cells in the row. This provides visual feedback on validation results in the Excel report.
functions.usage_example: highlight_validation(worksheet, row_cells)  # Highlights row based on validation results

functions.method_signature: def generate_report(data: list, output_dir: str, filename: str = 'validation_results')

parameters.name: data
parameters.description: List of dictionaries containing validation result data

parameters.name: output_dir
parameters.description: Directory where report files should be saved

parameters.name: filename
parameters.description: Base filename for the report files (without extension). Defaults to 'validation_results'.

functions.what_it_does: Generates both JSON and HTML validation reports from a list of result dictionaries. Creates the output directory if it does not exist using os.makedirs(). Constructs file paths for JSON and HTML files by joining output_dir with filename and appropriate extensions. Writes the data list to a JSON file with indent=2 for readability using json.dump(). Then generates an HTML report by writing HTML structure with a table. If data is not empty, extracts headers from the first dictionary's keys and writes them as table headers. Iterates through each row dictionary and writes table rows with values for each header. Closes the HTML tags. This provides both machine-readable (JSON) and human-readable (HTML) validation reports.

functions.method_signature: def write_excel_context(rows, wb, sheet_name, path, header, highlight_func=None)

parameters.name: rows
parameters.description: List of row data (dicts or lists) to write

parameters.name: wb
parameters.description: Openpyxl Workbook object

parameters.name: sheet_name
parameters.description: Name of the sheet to write to

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: File path to save the workbook

parameters.name: header
parameters.description: List of header column names

parameters.name: highlight_func
parameters.description: Optional function to call for highlighting each row. Receives (ws, row_cells, None) as arguments.

functions.what_it_does: Writes validation or scraping data rows to a specific Excel worksheet with header management, duplicate removal, and optional row highlighting. Gets or creates the sheet by name. If the sheet is empty (max_row==0), writes the header row and applies header fill color from Constants.COLOR_HEADER. If the sheet has data, validates that the first row matches the expected header, and if not, deletes the first row and writes the correct header. Identifies and collects indices of empty rows and duplicate header rows for deletion. Deletes these rows in reverse order to maintain correct indices. Iterates through the provided rows, skipping empty rows. For each row, if it's a dictionary, extracts values in header order; otherwise uses the row as-is. Appends the row to the sheet. If highlight_func is provided, calls it with the worksheet, the newly added row cells, and None. Finally saves the workbook to the specified path.

parameters.name: results
parameters.description: List of validation result dictionaries

parameters.name: wb
parameters.description: Openpyxl Workbook object

parameters.name: sheet_name
parameters.description: Name of the sheet to write to

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: File path to save the workbook

functions.what_it_does: Convenience wrapper function that writes validation results to an Excel worksheet with automatic highlighting. Calls write_excel_context() with the provided results, workbook, sheet_name, and path parameters. Passes Constants.VALIDATION_HEADERS as the header parameter to ensure consistent column structure across all validation reports. Passes highlight_validation as the highlight_func parameter to automatically apply color coding to validation result rows based on pass/fail status. This simplifies the common use case of writing validation reports with standard headers and highlighting.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: CamelCase string to convert

functions.docstring:
Convert CamelCase flow names to snake_case module/function names.
e.g. CreateV3Account -> create_v3_account
functions.what_it_does: Converts CamelCase strings to snake_case format for Python naming conventions. Uses two regular expression substitutions. First, re.sub() with pattern '(.)([A-Z][a-z]+)' inserts an underscore between a lowercase letter and an uppercase letter followed by lowercase letters (e.g., 'Create' -> 'Create_'). Second substitution with pattern '([a-z0-9])([A-Z])' inserts an underscore between a lowercase letter or digit and an uppercase letter (e.g., 'V3Account' -> 'V3_Account'). Finally converts the entire string to lowercase using lower(). Returns the snake_case string. This is used to convert flow class names like 'CreateV3Account' to module names like 'create_v3_account'.

parameters.name: flow_args
parameters.description: Dictionary of flow arguments

functions.what_it_does: Extracts and returns the language code from flow arguments dictionary. Uses dict.get() to retrieve the value associated with the key 'tenant_country_language_code' from the flow_args dictionary. Returns the language code string if present, or None if the key does not exist. This is a simple accessor function used throughout the framework to get the language/locale code for test execution.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: Target platform string from flow args

parameters.name: simulator_platform
parameters.description: Simulator platform string from flow args

functions.docstring: Get the target directory name based on target and simulator_platform parameters from flow args.
functions.what_it_does: Determines the appropriate target directory name based on target platform and simulator platform parameters. Defines a comprehensive mapping dictionary target_folder_mapping that maps various platform identifiers to standardized folder names (e.g., 'web_chrome' -> 'chrome', 'Android' -> 'android', 'GothamDesktop' -> 'wingotham', etc.). Checks if the target parameter starts with 'web_' using startswith(). If true, looks up the target in the mapping and returns the mapped value or the original target if not found. If target does not start with 'web_', looks up the simulator_platform in the mapping and returns the mapped value or the original target if not found. This standardizes directory naming across different platform configurations for organizing test outputs.

functions.method_signature: def run_flow(mode: str, flow_name: str, flow_args: Optional[Dict] = None, context_override: Optional[str] = None) -> None

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description: Execution mode: 'scrape', 'validate', or 'noop'

parameters.name: flow_name
parameters.description: Name of the test flow to execute

parameters.name: flow_args
parameters.description: Optional dictionary of flow arguments and configuration

parameters.name: context_override
parameters.description: Optional context name override (not used in current implementation)

functions.what_it_does: Main orchestration function that executes a test flow in scrape, validate, or noop mode with comprehensive setup, execution, and reporting. Validates that flow_name is provided. Converts flow_name from CamelCase to snake_case to construct the module path. Dynamically imports the flow module using importlib.import_module(). Verifies the module contains the expected function. Generates a run_id timestamp and extracts language code, target directory, and other parameters from flow_args. Based on the mode parameter, sets up appropriate output directories and defines a stage_callback function that either calls scrape_context, validate_context, or does nothing (noop). Initializes Config with all flow arguments. Optionally initializes ReportsManager for HTML/JSON reporting if not in scrape mode. Executes the flow function within a try-except-finally block, passing the stage_callback. Handles exceptions by logging errors and setting flow_success to False. In the finally block, finalizes the report if it exists. After execution, checks for validation failures or interruptions and raises SystemExit(1) if found. Logs completion message with output directory path. This is the central flow execution engine for the entire framework.

functions.method_signature: def run_scrape_mode(flow_name: str, flow_args: Dict) -> None

parameters.name: flow_name
parameters.description: Name of the test flow to scrape

parameters.name: flow_args
parameters.description: Dictionary of flow arguments

functions.what_it_does: Convenience wrapper function that executes a test flow in scrape mode. Simply calls run_flow() with mode parameter set to 'scrape', passing through the flow_name and flow_args parameters. This provides a cleaner API for callers who want to explicitly run scrape mode without having to specify the mode string.

functions.method_signature: def run_validate_mode(flow_name: str, flow_args: Dict) -> None

parameters.name: flow_name
parameters.description: Name of the test flow to validate

parameters.name: flow_args
parameters.description: Dictionary of flow arguments

functions.what_it_does: Convenience wrapper function that executes a test flow in validate mode. Simply calls run_flow() with mode parameter set to 'validate', passing through the flow_name and flow_args parameters. This provides a cleaner API for callers who want to explicitly run validate mode without having to specify the mode string.

functions.method_signature: def run_noop_mode(flow_name: str, flow_args: Dict) -> None

parameters.name: flow_name
parameters.description: Name of the test flow to run in noop mode

parameters.name: flow_args
parameters.description: Dictionary of flow arguments

functions.what_it_does: Convenience wrapper function that executes a test flow in noop (no operation) mode. Simply calls run_flow() with mode parameter set to 'noop', passing through the flow_name and flow_args parameters. In noop mode, the flow executes but does not perform any scraping or validation operations, useful for testing flow logic without side effects. This provides a cleaner API for callers who want to explicitly run noop mode without having to specify the mode string.

functions.method_signature: def load_json_file(file_path: str, encoding: str = 'utf-8') -> dict

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: Path to the JSON file to load

parameters.name: encoding
parameters.description: File encoding. Defaults to 'utf-8'.

functions.what_it_does: Loads and parses a JSON file, returning the parsed data as a dictionary with comprehensive error handling. Wraps file operations in try-except blocks. Opens the file at file_path with the specified encoding using a context manager. Parses the JSON content using json.load() and returns the resulting dictionary. If the file is not found, raises FileNotFoundError with a descriptive message including the file path. If JSON parsing fails, catches json.JSONDecodeError and raises ValueError with a descriptive message including the file path and the original error. This provides safe JSON file loading with clear error messages for debugging.
functions.usage_example: data = load_json_file('config/settings.json')  # Returns parsed JSON as dict

functions.what_it_does: Loads locale mapping data from a JSON file specified in Constants.LOCALE_MAP_PATH. Simply calls load_json_file() with the path from Constants.LOCALE_MAP_PATH and returns the parsed dictionary. This provides a convenient way to access locale/language configuration data used throughout the framework for internationalization and localization testing.
functions.usage_example: locale_data = load_locale_data()  # Returns locale mapping dictionary

functions.what_it_does: Loads printer profile configuration data from a JSON file specified in Constants.PRINTER_PROFILES_PATH. Simply calls load_json_file() with the path from Constants.PRINTER_PROFILES_PATH and returns the parsed dictionary. This provides access to printer model configurations, SKUs, and capabilities used for printer-specific test scenarios.
functions.usage_example: printer_profiles = load_printer_profiles()  # Returns printer profiles dictionary

parameters.name: country
parameters.description: Country name to get address data for

functions.what_it_does: Loads address data for a specific country from a JSON file. Calls load_json_file() with Constants.ADDRESS_DATA_PATH to load the complete address data dictionary. Then uses dict.get() to retrieve and return the address data for the specified country key. Returns the country-specific address dictionary if found, or None if the country is not in the data. This provides test data for address fields in country-specific formats.
functions.usage_example: us_address = load_address_for_country('United States')  # Returns US address data dict

functions.method_signature: def get_printer_details_for_country(profile_name: str, region: str, printer_profiles: dict, easy_enroll_flag: str = None, biz_model: str = 'Flex') -> tuple

parameters.name: profile_name
parameters.description: Name of the printer profile

parameters.name: region
parameters.description: Region code

parameters.name: printer_profiles
parameters.description: Dictionary of printer profiles loaded from JSON

parameters.name: easy_enroll_flag
parameters.description: Optional 'yes' or 'no' to filter by easy enroll capability

parameters.name: biz_model
parameters.description: Business model string. Defaults to 'Flex'.

functions.what_it_does: Retrieves printer details (profile name, SKU, supply variant, business model) for a specific printer profile and region with optional easy enroll filtering. First validates that profile_name exists in printer_profiles dictionary, raising ValueError if not found. Gets the SKUs dictionary for the profile and validates that the region exists, raising ValueError if not. Retrieves the list of SKUs for the region. If easy_enroll_flag is provided, converts it to boolean (checking if it equals 'yes' case-insensitively) and filters the SKUs list to only include SKUs where the 'easy_enroll' field matches the desired boolean value. Raises ValueError if no matching SKUs are found after filtering. Selects the first SKU from the (possibly filtered) list and extracts the 'sku' and 'supplyvariant' values. Returns a tuple of (profile_name, sku, supplyvariant, biz_model). This is used to get printer configuration details for test setup.

parameters.name: svg_xml
parameters.description: SVG XML content as string

parameters.name: fallback_duration_ms
parameters.description: Fallback duration in milliseconds if no duration found. Defaults to 1000.

functions.what_it_does: Extracts animation duration from SVG XML content by searching for SMIL dur attributes or CSS animation-duration properties. First uses regex to search for SMIL dur attribute pattern 'dur="value(ms|s)"'. If found, extracts the numeric value and unit, converts to milliseconds (dividing by 1000 if unit is 'ms', keeping as-is if 's'), multiplies by 1000 to get milliseconds, converts to int and returns. If SMIL pattern not found, searches for CSS animation-duration pattern 'animation-duration: value(ms|s)' and performs the same conversion. If neither pattern is found, returns the fallback_duration_ms value. This helps determine appropriate frame capture timing for animated SVGs.

functions.method_signature: def extract_animated_svgs(page, min_size=5120, poll_interval=0.02, max_frames=100, upscale_factor=4, save_debug_frames=True, debug_frame_dir='./debug_svg_frames')

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

parameters.name: min_size
parameters.description: Minimum SVG size in bytes to consider. Defaults to 5120.

parameters.name: poll_interval
parameters.description: Seconds between frame captures. Defaults to 0.02.

parameters.name: max_frames
parameters.description: Maximum frames to capture. Defaults to 100.

parameters.name: upscale_factor
parameters.description: Factor to upscale SVG for capture. Defaults to 4.

parameters.name: save_debug_frames
parameters.description: Whether to save individual debug frames. Defaults to True.

parameters.name: debug_frame_dir
parameters.description: Directory for debug frames. Defaults to './debug_svg_frames'.

functions.what_it_does: Comprehensive function that detects, captures, and converts animated SVG elements on a page to GIF format with extensive animation detection logic. Queries all SVG elements on the page. For each SVG: checks visibility, extracts outer HTML, validates size against min_size threshold. Detects animation using multiple strategies: SMIL tags (animate, animateTransform), CSS animations (animationName, transitionProperty), JavaScript timers, requestAnimationFrame, script tags, and event handlers. Performs hard reset of animations by removing and re-adding the element to the DOM. Triggers animations via hover and mouseenter events. Waits a fixed initial delay. Captures multiple detection frames and compares them pixel-by-pixel using PIL Image and ImageChops to detect visual changes. If animation detected, upscales the SVG by modifying width/height attributes. Captures frames at poll_interval until max_frames reached or animation loops (detected by comparing frame hash to first frame hash). Calculates per-frame duration based on loop period. Restores original SVG size. Converts frames to GIF using PIL with palette optimization and disposal mode 2. Returns list of dictionaries containing element handle, outer HTML, GIF bytes, frame count, resolution, animation type, and timing information. This is a highly sophisticated animation extraction system with extensive logging and debugging capabilities.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

parameters.name: min_size
parameters.description: Minimum GIF size in bytes. Defaults to 1024.

functions.what_it_does: Extracts animated GIF images from a web page by finding img elements with .gif sources and validating they are truly animated. Queries all img elements with src containing '.gif' using page.query_selector_all(). For each GIF element: checks visibility, gets the src attribute, validates it contains '.gif'. Makes an HTTP request to fetch the GIF bytes using page.context.request.get(). Validates response status is 200 and content size is at least min_size bytes. Opens the GIF bytes with PIL Image and checks if it has is_animated attribute set to True and n_frames > 1 to confirm it's actually animated (not a static GIF). If all validations pass, appends a dictionary to results containing the element handle, src URL, raw bytes, and size. Returns the list of animated GIF dictionaries. Wraps each element processing in try-except to continue on errors. This is used to capture existing animated GIFs for validation comparison.

functions.method_signature: def extract_animated_visuals(page, selector='.stepsImage', min_size=1024, screenshot_dir='.', context_hint=None, poll_interval=0.03, max_frames=60, detect_loop=True, save_debug_frames=False)

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

parameters.name: selector
parameters.description: CSS selector for elements to capture. Defaults to '.stepsImage'.

parameters.name: min_size
parameters.description: Minimum output size in bytes. Defaults to 1024.

parameters.name: screenshot_dir
parameters.description: Directory to save screenshots. Defaults to current directory.

parameters.name: context_hint
parameters.description: Optional context name for filename generation.

parameters.name: poll_interval
parameters.description: Seconds between frame captures. Defaults to 0.03.

parameters.name: max_frames
parameters.description: Maximum frames to capture. Defaults to 60.

parameters.name: detect_loop
parameters.description: Whether to detect animation loops. Defaults to True.

parameters.name: save_debug_frames
parameters.description: Whether to save individual debug frames. Defaults to False.

functions.what_it_does: Extracts animated visual elements (SVG, Canvas, Lottie, CSS sprites) from a page by capturing frame sequences and converting to GIF or PNG. Queries all elements matching the selector. For each element: checks visibility, gets inner HTML, determines animation type by checking for SVG tags, canvas elements, 'lottie' text, or CSS background images. Generates a filename using context_hint and animation type. Captures frames in a loop up to max_frames: forces repaint by setting random outline style, scrolls element into view, takes screenshot, opens as PIL Image, computes SHA256 hash of image bytes, optionally saves debug frame PNG. If detect_loop is True and frame hash matches first frame hash after 3 frames, breaks loop indicating animation has looped. Determines if animated by checking for multiple unique frame hashes. If animated with 2+ frames, calculates duration per frame based on loop period if detected, converts frames to palette mode, saves as GIF with calculated duration and loop=0. If not animated, saves first frame as PNG. Reads the output file bytes. If size is less than min_size, deletes the file and skips. Appends result dictionary with element, animation_type, screenshot_path, is_animated, frames_captured, duration_per_frame_ms, size, and tag. Returns list of results. This provides flexible animation capture for various animation technologies with loop detection and automatic GIF/PNG selection.

file_analyses.analysis_notes: This is a comprehensive core utilities module that serves as the foundation for the entire AURA test automation framework. It contains 50+ utility functions organized into several categories: basic utilities (timestamps, string sanitization, random generation, hashing), Excel operations (workbook/sheet management, row operations, column width adjustment), Playwright browser interactions (element visibility checks, position/style extraction, text extraction, scroll control, screenshot capture), validation reporting (Excel highlighting, HTML/JSON report generation), flow execution orchestration (the complex run_flow function that handles scrape/validate/noop modes), data loading (JSON files, locale data, printer profiles, addresses), and advanced animation extraction (SVG, GIF, and general visual animations with sophisticated detection and GIF conversion). The extract_animated_svgs function is particularly notable for its extensive animation detection logic covering SMIL, CSS, JavaScript, and pixel-diff strategies with hard resets, hover triggers, loop detection, and frame hashing. The run_flow function is the central orchestration engine that dynamically imports flow modules, sets up directories, initializes configuration, executes flows with callbacks, handles reporting, and manages error states. The ElementsDict class provides convenient attribute-style access to dictionary keys. This module is heavily used by scraper.py, validator.py, and all test flow modules.

file_analyses.file_purpose: Core validation module responsible for comparing live UI against baseline scraped data. Performs text validation, screenshot comparison with pixel-diff analysis, exclusion region handling, suggested exclusion generation, animation validation (SVG and GIF), full-page screenshot validation, and Excel report generation with color-coded results. Supports screenshot-only mode for quick checkpoint captures without element-level validation.

imports.import_statement: from core.utils import sanitize_filename, load_or_create_workbook, save_workbook, highlight_validation, save_full_page_screenshot, take_element_screenshot, take_element_handle_screenshot, hash_text, ensure_dirs, write_report_excel_context, extract_animated_svgs, extract_animated_gifs

parameters.name: exclude_str
parameters.description: String representation of exclusion regions, typically from Excel cell

functions.what_it_does: Parses a string representation of exclusion regions into a list of tuples. If exclude_str is empty or None, returns an empty list. Otherwise, uses ast.literal_eval() to safely parse the string as a Python literal (list or tuple). If the result is a single tuple (e.g., '(10, 20, 100, 50)'), wraps it in a list to ensure consistent list-of-tuples format. Returns the list of exclusion tuples where each tuple is (x, y, width, height). If parsing fails due to invalid syntax, catches the exception and returns an empty list. This is used to parse exclusion region data stored as strings in Excel cells.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: X coordinate of the pixel

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: Y coordinate of the pixel

parameters.name: exclusions
parameters.description: List of exclusion region tuples (x, y, width, height)

functions.what_it_does: Checks if a pixel coordinate (x, y) falls within any of the defined exclusion regions. Iterates through each exclusion tuple in the exclusions list. For each exclusion, unpacks the tuple as (ex_x, ex_y, ex_w, ex_h) representing the top-left corner and dimensions of the exclusion rectangle. Checks if the pixel x coordinate is within the range [ex_x, ex_x + ex_w) and the y coordinate is within [ex_y, ex_y + ex_h). If both conditions are true, the pixel is inside this exclusion region and the function returns True immediately. If no exclusion region contains the pixel after checking all regions, returns False. This is used during image comparison to determine if a differing pixel should be ignored because it's in an excluded area.

parameters.name: pixels
parameters.description: List of (x, y) pixel coordinates representing failed pixels

parameters.name: size
parameters.description: Tuple of (width, height) representing image dimensions

functions.what_it_does: Generates bounding box rectangles around clusters of failed pixels using flood-fill algorithm. If pixels list is empty, returns empty list. Creates a binary mask image using PIL Image.new() with mode 'L' (grayscale) initialized to 0 (black). Sets all failed pixel coordinates to 255 (white) in the mask. Initializes a visited set to track processed pixels and an empty boxes list. Defines an inner flood_fill function that performs iterative flood-fill starting from a seed pixel using a stack-based approach. The flood-fill explores connected white pixels (value 255) in 4 directions (left, right, up, down), tracking the minimum and maximum x and y coordinates to determine the bounding box. Returns a tuple (x, y, width, height) for the bounding box. Iterates through all pixels in the mask row by row. For each white pixel not yet visited, calls flood_fill to get its bounding box. Only adds boxes to the result list if both width and height are at least MIN_TOLERANCE_PIXELS (defined as constant 1). Returns the list of bounding box tuples. This is used to generate suggested exclusion regions for validation failures by grouping nearby failed pixels into rectangular regions.

parameters.name: img1_path
parameters.description: Path to the baseline image

parameters.name: img2_path
parameters.description: Path to the validation image

parameters.name: exclusions
parameters.description: List of exclusion region tuples

parameters.name: color_tolerance
parameters.description: Maximum color difference threshold. Defaults to COLOR_TOLERANCE constant (35).

parameters.name: original_exclude_str
parameters.description: Original exclusion string from Excel. If '[]', returns pass immediately.

functions.what_it_does: Performs pixel-by-pixel image comparison with exclusion region filtering and bounding box generation for failures. First checks if either image file does not exist, returning (False, None, [], []) if missing. If original_exclude_str is '[]' (empty exclusion list as string), returns (True, None, [], []) immediately treating it as a pass. Opens both images and converts to RGB mode. Determines the maximum width and height between the two images. Creates two new white background images of the maximum dimensions and pastes the original images at (0, 0), effectively padding smaller images with white. Initializes two lists: failed_pixels for all differing pixels and failed_pixels_outside_exclusions for pixels that differ and are not in exclusion regions. Iterates through every pixel coordinate in the padded images. For each pixel, gets RGB values from both images, calculates Euclidean distance in RGB space using sum of squared differences then square root. If the distance exceeds color_tolerance, adds the pixel to failed_pixels. Additionally checks if the pixel is not in any exclusion region using is_in_exclusion(); if not excluded, adds to failed_pixels_outside_exclusions. After processing all pixels, calls get_bounding_boxes() with failed_pixels_outside_exclusions to generate suggested exclusion regions. If no boxes were generated (all failures are within exclusions or no failures), returns (True, None, [], failed_pixels) indicating pass. Otherwise returns (False, None, boxes, failed_pixels_outside_exclusions) indicating failure with suggested exclusions. This is the core image comparison function that determines validation pass/fail status.

parameters.name: img2_path
parameters.description: Path to the validation image

parameters.name: failed_pixels_outside_exclusions
parameters.description: List of (x, y) coordinates of failed pixels not in exclusions

parameters.name: out_path
parameters.description: Path where the overlay image should be saved

parameters.name: alpha
parameters.description: Alpha transparency value for the red overlay (0-255). Defaults to 120.

functions.what_it_does: Creates a visual diff image by overlaying red semi-transparent pixels on failed areas of the validation image. Opens the validation image at img2_path and converts to RGBA mode to support transparency. Creates a new transparent overlay image of the same size with all pixels set to transparent red (255, 0, 0, 0). Gets direct pixel access to the overlay using load(). Extracts the image dimensions. Iterates through each failed pixel coordinate in failed_pixels_outside_exclusions. For each coordinate, validates it's within image bounds (0 <= x < width and 0 <= y < height). If valid, sets the overlay pixel at that coordinate to semi-transparent red (255, 0, 0, alpha). After processing all failed pixels, composites the overlay onto the validation image using Image.alpha_composite() which blends the semi-transparent red overlay with the original image. Saves the resulting composite image to out_path. Returns True on success. This creates a visual representation of validation failures where failed pixels are highlighted in red, making it easy to see exactly where differences occurred.

functions.method_signature: def compare_gif_files(gif_path_1: str, gif_path_2: str) -> bool

parameters.name: gif_path_1
parameters.description: Path to the first GIF file

parameters.name: gif_path_2
parameters.description: Path to the second GIF file

functions.docstring: Compare two GIF files byte-wise. Returns True if identical.
functions.what_it_does: Performs byte-by-byte comparison of two GIF files to determine if they are identical. First checks if both files exist using os.path.exists(). If either file is missing, logs a warning with both file paths and returns False. Opens both files in binary read mode using context managers. Reads the complete content of both files using read() and compares the byte strings using equality operator. Returns True if the byte content is identical, False otherwise. This is used for validating animated GIF elements by comparing the baseline GIF captured during scraping with the GIF captured during validation.
functions.usage_example: is_same = compare_gif_files('baseline.gif', 'validation.gif')  # Returns True if identical

functions.method_signature: def compare_svg_xml(svg_xml_1: str, svg_xml_2: str) -> bool

parameters.name: svg_xml_1
parameters.description: First SVG XML content as string

parameters.name: svg_xml_2
parameters.description: Second SVG XML content as string

functions.docstring:
Robust SVG comparison: ignores whitespace, attribute order, float precision, and comment nodes.
Handles: attribute sorting, rounds floats, strips comments and normalizes text/tail.
functions.what_it_does: Performs robust semantic comparison of two SVG XML strings by normalizing and comparing their structure rather than raw text. Defines inner function normalize_float() that attempts to round float values to 3 decimal places, handling both single floats and space/comma-separated float lists (common in SVG path data). Defines clean_element() recursive function that normalizes an XML element by: sorting attributes alphabetically, normalizing float values in attributes, stripping whitespace from text and tail, removing comment nodes, recursively cleaning child elements, and sorting children by tag name, attributes, and text for deterministic order. Defines get_root() helper that parses XML string using ElementTree.fromstring(), catching parse errors and logging warnings. Parses both SVG strings to get root elements. If either parse fails, falls back to whitespace-insensitive string comparison using regex to remove all whitespace. If both parse successfully, calls clean_element() on both roots to normalize them. Serializes both normalized trees back to strings using ElementTree.tostring(). Compares the normalized strings and returns True if identical, False otherwise. This handles common SVG variations like attribute order differences, float precision differences, whitespace variations, and comment presence, focusing on semantic equivalence rather than exact text matching.
functions.usage_example: is_same = compare_svg_xml(baseline_svg, validation_svg)  # Returns True if semantically equivalent

functions.method_signature: def validate_context(flow_name, context_name, page, excel_path, output_folder, validation_workbook_path=None, screenshot_only=False, sub_context=None, lang_code=None, animations=False, target_dir=None)

parameters.name: flow_name
parameters.description: Name of the test flow

parameters.name: context_name
parameters.description: Name of the context/checkpoint being validated

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

parameters.name: excel_path
parameters.description: Path to the baseline Excel file with scraped data

parameters.name: output_folder
parameters.description: Directory for validation outputs

parameters.name: validation_workbook_path
parameters.description: Optional path for validation results workbook. Auto-generated if None.

parameters.name: screenshot_only
parameters.description: If True, only captures full-page screenshot without element validation.

parameters.name: sub_context
parameters.description: Optional sub-context name for granular checkpoints

parameters.name: lang_code
parameters.description: Language code for filename generation

parameters.name: animations
parameters.description: If True, validates animated SVG and GIF elements

parameters.name: target_dir
parameters.description: Target directory name for organizing outputs

functions.what_it_does: Main validation function that compares live page state against baseline scraped data with comprehensive element, text, screenshot, and animation validation. Creates output directories for validation screenshots, failure screenshots, and animations. Constructs subcontext suffix for filenames. If screenshot_only mode: captures full-page screenshot with retry logic (3 attempts with 2s delay), loads baseline Excel to find matching full-page baseline, performs image comparison with exclusion handling, generates validation report row with pass/fail status and suggested exclusions if failed, creates overlay diff image if failed, writes report to Excel, and returns early. For full validation mode: creates or loads validation workbook, loads baseline Excel and finds the correct sheet, extracts header row, filters baseline data rows matching context and sub_context. If animations flag is True: extracts all animated SVGs from page using extract_animated_svgs(), saves each SVG to validation_animations folder with hash-based filename. Queries all visible elements on the page with text content and valid bounding boxes, storing them in live_elements list. Iterates through each baseline row: handles animation type rows by extracting and comparing GIF or SVG animations, handles fullpage type rows by capturing and comparing full-page screenshots, handles regular element rows by finding matching live elements by text and tag, validating text and href matches, capturing element screenshots, performing image comparison with exclusions, generating suggested exclusion boxes on failure, creating overlay diff images. For each row, constructs a detailed validation result dictionary with context, timestamp, expected/actual values, pass/fail status, screenshot paths, diff images, exclusion regions, and comments. Writes all results to Excel using write_report_excel_context() with automatic highlighting. Logs completion message. This is the core validation engine that orchestrates all validation operations for a single context checkpoint.

file_analyses.analysis_notes: This is the core validation module that performs comprehensive comparison of live UI against baseline data. The validate_context function is the main entry point and orchestrates all validation operations. It supports multiple validation modes: screenshot-only for quick checkpoints, full element-level validation with text and screenshot comparison, and animation validation for SVG and GIF elements. The image comparison logic is sophisticated, using pixel-by-pixel RGB distance calculation with configurable color tolerance (default 35), exclusion region filtering to ignore known dynamic areas, and automatic generation of suggested exclusion regions using flood-fill bounding box detection. The compare_svg_xml function provides robust semantic SVG comparison that normalizes attribute order, float precision, whitespace, and comments. The module integrates tightly with core.utils for screenshot capture, Excel operations, and animation extraction. Validation results are written to Excel with automatic color highlighting (green for pass, red for fail, yellow for pass with exclusions) and include detailed information about expected vs actual values, screenshot paths, diff images, and suggested exclusions. The screenshot-only mode with retry logic (3 attempts) handles transient page loading issues. Animation validation extracts all SVGs once and compares them by hash-based filename matching. The module handles sub-contexts for granular checkpoints and supports language-specific and target-specific output organization.

file_analyses.file_purpose: HTML help documentation file providing comprehensive usage instructions, parameter descriptions, mode explanations, examples, and troubleshooting information for the AURA CLI test automation framework. Serves as user-facing documentation for running scrape, validate, and noop modes with various configuration options.
file_analyses.analysis_notes: This is a static HTML documentation file that provides user-facing help for the AURA CLI. It contains comprehensive documentation organized into sections: Usage (command syntax), Parameters (detailed parameter descriptions including --flow, --mode, --stack, --locale, --language, --printer_profile, --easy_enroll, --biz_model, --headless, --screenshot_only, --target, --debug), Modes (scrape, validate, noop explanations), Contexts & Subcontexts (checkpoint naming conventions), Screenshot-Only (quick checkpoint mode), Examples (multiple real-world command examples), Calling from Flow Code (stage_callback usage), Output (directory structure and file locations), Notes (best practices and tips), and Troubleshooting & Support. The document uses CSS styling for readability with code blocks, pre-formatted text, and note boxes. It documents the --target parameter with all supported OSS platforms (web, wingotham, macgotham, android, ios, hpxwindows, hpxmac, hpxandroid, hpxios). The examples section shows practical commands for different scenarios like scraping in different locales, validating with printer profiles, targeting specific platforms, and using language codes. The document also explains the --addflow parameter for creating new flow structures. This file would typically be opened in a browser or served via a local web server to provide interactive help to users. It does not contain any executable code or locators, only documentation content.

file_analyses.file_purpose: Helper module providing utility methods for subscription cancellation flow testing. Contains static methods for selecting cancellation feedback options, verifying cancellation UI elements, validating cancellation timeline, handling plan changes during cancellation, managing subscription restoration, and testing various cancellation-related features including pause plan, transfer subscription, and paper subscription cancellation.

imports.import_statement: from datetime import datetime, time, timedelta

classes.class_purpose: Static helper class providing utility methods for testing subscription cancellation flows. Contains methods for selecting cancellation feedback options (both ink and paper), verifying cancellation UI elements and sections, validating cancellation timeline steps and dates, handling plan changes during cancellation, managing subscription restoration, testing pause plan functionality, and validating various cancellation-related features. All methods are static and do not require instantiation.
classes.how_to_instantiate: CancellationPlanHelper is used via static methods - no instantiation needed. Call CancellationPlanHelper.select_cancellation_feedback_option(page, 'I do not print enough') directly.

functions.method_signature: def select_cancellation_feedback_option(page, feedback_option='I do not print enough', submit: bool = True)

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

parameters.name: feedback_option
parameters.description: Feedback option text to select. Defaults to 'I do not print enough'.

parameters.name: submit
parameters.description: Whether to submit the feedback or return to account. Defaults to True.

functions.what_it_does: Selects a cancellation feedback option for ink subscription cancellation and optionally submits or returns to account. Creates CancellationPage instance. Clicks the continue button with 120s timeout. Defines option_map dictionary mapping feedback text to radio button selectors for options: 'I do not print enough', 'The service is too expensive', 'I have not received my shipment', 'I have replaced my printer', 'Wi-Fi issues with my printer', and 'Other'. Gets the selector for the specified feedback_option from the map, raising ValueError if option not found. Clicks the radio button using page.click() with force=True. If feedback_option is 'Other', fills the feedback text box with 'Other Reason'. If submit parameter is True, clicks the submit feedback button; otherwise clicks the return to account button. This automates the feedback selection step of the cancellation flow.

functions.method_signature: def select_paper_cancellation_feedback_option(page, feedback_option="I'm worried I'm going to run out of paper", submit: bool = True)

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

parameters.name: feedback_option
parameters.default_value: "I'm worried I'm going to run out of paper"
parameters.description: Paper cancellation feedback option text. Defaults to "I'm worried I'm going to run out of paper".

parameters.name: submit
parameters.description: Whether to submit or return to account. Defaults to True.

functions.what_it_does: Selects a cancellation feedback option specifically for paper subscription cancellation with paper-specific feedback reasons. Creates CancellationPage instance and clicks continue button with 120s timeout. Defines option_map with paper-specific feedback options including: replenishment anxiety, leakage, did not print enough, ran out of paper, paper overages, temporary cancellation, stockpiling, too expensive, damaged paper, did not receive shipment, moved to non-service area, paper quality, and Other. Gets the selector for the specified feedback_option, raising ValueError if not found. Clicks the radio button with force=True. If 'Other' is selected, fills the feedback text box with 'Other Reason'. If submit is True, clicks submit feedback button; otherwise clicks return to account button. This handles the paper-specific cancellation feedback flow which has different options than ink cancellation.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

parameters.name: section_title
parameters.description: Title of the retention section to verify

functions.what_it_does: Verifies that a specific retention option section is visible and properly structured on the cancellation page. Creates CancellationPage instance. Defines link_selectors dictionary mapping section titles to their corresponding link selectors: 'Have your printing needs changed?' to change_plan_link, 'Pause your subscription for up to 6 months' to pause_plan_link, 'Did you get a new printer?' to transfer_subscription_link, 'Have questions or need help?' to contact_hp_support_link, and 'Only pay when you need to print' to only_pay_when_you_need_to_print_link. Gets the selector for the specified section_title, raising ValueError if not found. Locates the retention option container by navigating up two parent levels from the link using xpath. Locates the image, text paragraph (last p element), and button within the container. Verifies that the image, text, and button are all visible using expect().to_be_visible(). Verifies that the text content matches the section_title using expect().to_have_text(). This ensures retention options are properly displayed with all required elements.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

functions.what_it_does: Verifies that the cancellation timeline page displays all required elements. Creates CancellationTimelinePage instance. Uses expect() with 90s timeout to verify header_icon is visible. Verifies header_title, header_subtitle, restore_subscription button, and whats_happens_next section are all visible. The timeline_img verification is commented out. This ensures the cancellation timeline page has loaded correctly with all key UI elements present.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

functions.what_it_does: Verifies the cancellation timeline steps with date validation and tolerance for date ranges. Creates CancellationTimelinePage instance. Gets today's date and formats as 'MMM DD, YYYY'. Extracts the last day date from step 1 text using regex to find date pattern. Parses the last day date and calculates final bill date as last_day + 1 day. Verifies step 1 (cancellation submitted) with today's date in title. Verifies step 2 (last day to print) with last_day date in title. For step 3 (cartridges deactivated), uses regex pattern to match a date range format allowing for the final bill date to vary by ±1 day to handle timezone and processing variations. Verifies step 4 and step 5 icons and content are visible. This validates the timeline shows correct dates with appropriate tolerance for date calculation variations.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

functions.what_it_does: Verifies the 'Change Your Mind' section on the cancellation timeline page with both keep subscription and transfer subscription options. Creates CancellationTimelinePage instance. Verifies keep_subscription_as_it_was element is visible with 90s timeout. Uses XPath to locate the retention option text and link elements within the keep subscription card structure. Verifies the text is visible and contains 'Restore subscription?'. Verifies the link is visible. Then locates the transfer subscription retention option text and link using XPath. Verifies the text is visible and contains 'Did you get a new printer?'. Verifies the link is visible. This ensures both retention options in the change your mind section are properly displayed with correct text and clickable links.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

functions.what_it_does: Verifies the initial state of the cancellation feedback page including title, subtitle, radio buttons, text box, and button states. Creates CancellationPage instance. Verifies cancellation_feedback_title and cancellation_feedback_subtitle are visible. Asserts that all six radio buttons (do not print enough, wifi issues, service too expensive, did not receive shipment, have replaced printer, other) are not checked initially using page.is_checked(). Verifies feedback_text_box is visible and has disabled attribute set (cannot be edited until a radio is selected). Verifies submit_feedback_button is visible and has disabled attribute set (cannot be clicked until feedback is provided). Verifies return_to_account_button is visible and does not have disabled attribute (can be clicked immediately). This validates the initial state of the feedback form before any user interaction.

file_analyses.analysis_notes: This is a comprehensive helper module for testing subscription cancellation flows with 35+ static methods. It handles both ink and paper subscription cancellation scenarios with separate feedback option methods. The module extensively uses Playwright's expect() assertions for verification. Key functionality includes: selecting cancellation feedback with option mapping dictionaries, verifying UI sections and retention options, validating cancellation timeline with date calculations and regex patterns for flexible date matching, handling plan changes during cancellation (change plan modal, billing cycle selection, plan selection), managing subscription restoration and pause functionality, verifying cancellation banners and timeline modals, testing various cancellation-related links and buttons, and validating radio button behavior and form states. The date validation logic is sophisticated, using datetime manipulation and regex patterns to handle timezone variations and date range formats. The module integrates with multiple page objects (CancellationPage, CancellationTimelinePage, CancellationBannerPage, ChangePlanCancellationPage, OverviewPage, UpdatePlanPage) to provide comprehensive cancellation flow testing capabilities. Many methods include detailed XPath navigation to locate nested elements within complex UI structures. The verify_the_steps_on_cancellation_timeline_page method demonstrates advanced date handling with tolerance for ±1 day variations in final bill dates.

file_analyses.file_purpose: Comprehensive helper module for dashboard-related test operations including dashboard access, first-time setup, status card verification, plan details validation, progress bar checks, plan management (pause/resume), billing and shipping operations, payment method handling (credit card, PayPal, Google Pay, Apple Pay, direct debit), printer selection, footer validation, tour modal handling, notification verification, and various dashboard UI element interactions. Provides extensive utility methods for testing all aspects of the HP Instant Ink dashboard.

classes.class_purpose: Comprehensive static helper class providing utility methods for all dashboard-related test operations. Contains methods for dashboard access and navigation, first-time setup with modal handling, status card and plan details verification, progress bar validation, plan management (pause/resume), billing and shipping address operations, payment method handling for multiple payment types (credit cards via PGS and 2CO, PayPal, Google Pay, Apple Pay, direct debit), printer selection and management, footer link validation, tour modal handling, notification verification, savings calculator checks, session management, and various dashboard UI interactions. All methods are static and do not require instantiation.
classes.how_to_instantiate: DashboardHelper is used via static methods - no instantiation needed. Call DashboardHelper.access(page, 'user@example.com') directly.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

parameters.name: tenant_email
parameters.description: Optional email for sign-in if login page is encountered

functions.what_it_does: Navigates to the dashboard portal and handles login if needed. Waits for page load state with 15s timeout. Navigates to the portal shell URL from common._portalshell_url with 45s timeout. Waits for load state again. Attempts to wait for networkidle state, catching and ignoring exceptions if it times out. Checks if 'login' is in the current page URL. If login page is detected and tenant_email is not provided, raises ValueError requiring email for sign-in. If email is provided, calls HPIDHelper.sign_in() to perform authentication. Creates DashboardSideMenuPage instance and gets the visible Instant Ink menu link element. Asserts that the menu link is visible, confirming successful dashboard access. This is the primary method for navigating to and accessing the dashboard with automatic login handling.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

parameters.name: tenant_email
parameters.description: Email address for dashboard access

functions.what_it_does: Handles first-time dashboard access by dismissing all initial modals and popups. Calls DashboardHelper.access() to navigate to dashboard. Creates OverviewPage instance. Calls accept_banner_and_access_overview_page() to handle privacy banner. Defines a list of element lambda functions for modals that may appear on first access: special savings modal close, flip modal not now button, continue setting preferences, accept all preferences, no paper offer modal, skip tour, and another special savings modal close. If stack is 'pie' or 'stage', appends an additional special savings modal close to the list. Loops up to 6 times attempting to dismiss modals. In each iteration, creates a copy of the remaining elements list and iterates through each element function. For each element, tries to get the element, check if it's visible, and click it if visible. If successful, removes the element function from the list and logs which element was clicked. Catches and ignores exceptions for elements that are not present. Sleeps 2 seconds between iterations. This ensures a clean dashboard state by dismissing all first-time user experience modals.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

parameters.name: card_type
parameters.description: Optional card type identifier for payment data lookup

functions.what_it_does: Adds credit card billing information using the PGS (Payment Gateway Service) payment provider with 3DS authentication support. Creates ConfirmationPage and ShippingBillingPage instances. Gets payment data for the specified card_type using common.get_payment_method_data(). Logs the card type being used. If country code is IT (Italy), fills the tax_id_input with a test tax ID. Checks if the billing continue button is enabled, then clicks it. Waits for the PGS iframe to be visible with 120s timeout. Locates the iframe and gets a frame locator. Within the iframe, fills the card number input with the credit card number from payment data. Selects the expiration month and year from dropdowns. Types the CVV into the CVV input. Checks if a billing next button exists in the iframe; if so, clicks it. Otherwise, clicks the SCA save button to proceed to 3DS authentication. Attempts to locate the 3DS1 redirect iframe with 5s timeout; if found, uses that frame and sets submit selector to the 3DS1 submit button. If not found, waits for the 2FA iframe and uses that with the ACS submit selector. Within the challenge frame, waits for the authentication result dropdown to be visible with 10s timeout. Selects the authentication result from payment data (defaults to 'AUTHENTICATED'). Waits for the submit button to be visible and clicks it to complete authentication. Waits for the update shipping billing message to appear with 60s timeout, confirming successful billing addition. This handles the complete PGS payment flow including Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) / 3D Secure.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

parameters.name: card_type
parameters.description: Optional card type identifier for payment data lookup

functions.docstring: Handle 2Checkout payment method for billing page
functions.what_it_does: Adds credit card billing information using the 2Checkout (2CO) payment provider. Creates ShippingBillingPage and ConfirmationPage instances. Gets payment data for the specified card_type. Logs the card type being used. Checks if billing continue button is enabled and clicks it. Waits for the 2CO iframe to be visible with 120s timeout. Within the 2CO iframe, waits for and clicks the 'continue to billing' button with 60s timeout. Waits for and clicks the 'continue to payment' button with 60s timeout. Waits for the card name field to be attached with 3s timeout. Checks if the card_name_2co field is visible; if so, fills it with 'John Doe'. If not visible, waits for first_name_2co field, types 'John', presses Tab, waits for last_name_2co field, and types 'Doe'. Presses Tab to move to card number field. Types the credit card number from payment data into card_number_2co. Presses Tab and types the expiration date in MM/YY format into exp_date_2co. Presses Tab and types the CVV into cvv_2co. Waits for the place order button to be visible with 60s timeout and clicks it. Waits for the update shipping billing message to appear with 60s timeout, confirming successful billing addition. Logs success message. This handles the complete 2Checkout payment flow with its multi-step iframe navigation.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

functions.what_it_does: Verifies all footer links on the overview page by clicking each link and validating the destination URL. Creates OverviewPage instance. For HP.com link: expects a popup, clicks the footer_hp_com link, gets the popup page, verifies the URL contains '/home.html' with 30s timeout, and closes the popup. For Wireless Print Center: expects popup, clicks footer_wireless_print_center, verifies URL contains '/wireless-printing', closes popup. For Help Center: clicks footer_help_center, verifies the current page URL contains '/help/about-hp-smart', navigates back. For Terms of Use: expects popup, clicks footer_terms_of_use, verifies URL contains '/tou', closes popup. For HP Privacy: expects popup, clicks footer_hp_privacy, verifies URL contains '/privacy-central.html', closes popup. For HP Privacy Choices: expects popup, clicks footer_hp_your_privacy_choices, verifies URL contains '/your-privacy-choices.html', closes popup. This comprehensive test ensures all footer links are functional and navigate to the correct destinations, handling both same-page navigation and popup windows.

file_analyses.analysis_notes: This is an extremely comprehensive dashboard helper module with 70+ static methods covering virtually every aspect of dashboard testing. It demonstrates sophisticated payment handling with support for multiple payment gateways (PGS with 3DS authentication, 2Checkout with multi-step iframe navigation, PayPal with popup handling, direct debit). The module extensively uses GlobalState for accessing test data like addresses and payment methods. The first_access method shows advanced modal handling with a retry loop and lambda functions for flexible element detection. The billing methods demonstrate complex iframe navigation and authentication flow handling. The module integrates with 15+ page objects and multiple helper modules (HPIDHelper, UpdatePlanHelper, EnrollmentHelper). Many methods include detailed date validation logic with timedelta calculations and regex patterns. The verify_footer_section method shows comprehensive link validation with popup handling. The module handles country-specific variations (e.g., Italy tax ID, countries without city/state/zip mandates). The add_shipping method demonstrates sophisticated address form filling with country-specific field handling and suggested address modal handling. The module includes extensive logging using framework_logger for debugging. The payment methods support both PGS and 2CO payment gateways with automatic detection based on GlobalState.card_payment_gateway. The module also handles printer selection, notification verification, savings calculator validation, session expiration, and various dashboard UI interactions. This is a critical helper module that would be heavily used across all dashboard-related test scenarios.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object

parameters.name: payment_method
parameters.description: Optional payment method identifier. If None, uses GlobalState.payment_method.

file_analyses.what_it_does: Routes to the appropriate billing method handler based on payment method type. If payment_method is None, gets it from GlobalState.payment_method. Checks if payment_method contains 'paypal'; if so, calls add_paypal_billing() and returns. Checks if payment_method is 'google_pay'; if so, calls add_google_pay_billing() and returns. Checks if payment_method is 'apple_pay'; if so, calls add_apple_pay_billing() and returns. Checks if payment_method contains 'direct_debit'; if so, calls add_direct_debit_billing() and returns. If none of the above, gets card_payment_gateway from GlobalState. If gateway is 'PGS', calls add_pgs_billing(). If gateway is '2CO', calls add_2co_billing(). Otherwise raises ValueError for unsupported payment provider. This is the main entry point for adding billing that automatically routes to the correct payment handler based on payment method type and gateway configuration.
